verificationNote: Requirements constructed from IEC 62443, NIST SP 800-82, and NACSA Act 854 obligations. Not endorsed by any standards body — verify against authoritative sources before use in formal assessments.
name: Physical Security
description: Physical access controls for OT environments. Physical security is the foundation of OT security — all network controls can be bypassed if an attacker has physical access to a PLC cabinet or engineering workstation. Field sites (remote RTUs, unmanned substations, pipeline valve stations) are particularly vulnerable.

requirements.name: Physical access control to OT assets
requirements.description: OT systems must be physically secured with access controls appropriate to the criticality of the assets. SIS controllers require the highest level of physical security.

technical.summary: Layered physical security: facility perimeter, OT area access, control room access, individual cabinet access. Each layer adds friction for unauthorised access.
actions: Control room: card access control (individual access cards — not a door code shared by all)
PLC/DCS cabinet rooms: locked cabinets, limited key access
SIS cabinet room: additional access control beyond DCS room; access log mandatory
Field sites (RTUs, unmanned substations): perimeter fencing, cabinet locks, tamper detection
Visitor escort policy in all OT areas
CCTV in control rooms, cabinet rooms, and facility entry points
Physical access log for SIS area maintained manually

governance.summary: Physical access rights reviewed quarterly. Access removed immediately when personnel leave or change role.
actions: Quarterly physical access review
Immediate removal on personnel departure
Annual physical security audit

slMapping.sl2: Card access control; visitor escort; CCTV
slMapping.sl3: Perimeter + room + cabinet layered access; access logs; automatic alert on after-hours access
slMapping.sl4: SIS room requires dual-person access; physical tamper detection on SIS cabinets; biometric option

requirements.name: Field site physical security
requirements.description: Remote OT field sites (substations, pump stations, RTU sites, pipeline valve stations) are typically unmanned and present unique physical security challenges. These sites are often targeted for initial access to larger OT networks.

technical.summary: Remote sites require physical tamper detection, secure enclosures, and detection of unauthorised entry linked to central monitoring.
actions: Perimeter fencing for remote sites where feasible
Tamper-evident seals or tamper detection sensors on RTU/field device cabinets
Camera/motion detection at unmanned sites with alert to control room or security centre
Cellular modem cabinets locked separately from process control equipment
Physical inspection of remote sites on scheduled rotation (frequency based on criticality)
Intrusion alarm linked to SCADA operator workstation or security monitoring centre

governance.summary: Field site physical security standard with inspection schedule. Tamper events escalated to OT security team.
actions: Regular site inspection schedule based on criticality
Tamper event log maintained
Annual physical security audit of remote sites

slMapping.sl3: Tamper detection; camera/alarm; inspection schedule; tamper event alerting
slMapping.sl4: Continuous tamper monitoring with immediate alert; armed response for highest-criticality remote sites

requirements.name: Personnel security for OT
requirements.description: Personnel with access to OT systems — employees and contractors — represent a significant insider threat vector. Background screening, security awareness training, and clear termination procedures reduce the risk of insider-initiated or insider-facilitated OT incidents.

legal.summary: NACSA Act 854 s18 requires cybersecurity measures appropriate to the nature and sensitivity of NCII. For OT environments, personnel security is a core component of those measures. IEC 62443-2-1 covers the personnel security management requirements for OT security programs.

technical.summary: Background screening prior to OT access. OT security awareness training at onboarding and annually. Immediate access removal on departure. Monitoring of personnel accessing safety systems.
actions: Background screening for all personnel with unescorted access to OT control rooms, cabinet rooms, and SIS areas (criminal check, employment history verification)
OT security awareness training at onboarding: what OT security means, what phishing looks like for OT operators, USB policy, vendor interaction policy, how to report a security concern
Annual OT security awareness refresher — include recent OT incidents (e.g., Oldsmar water, Colonial Pipeline) to make training relevant
Maintain list of personnel with OT access rights — review quarterly against HR records
Immediate process on departure or role change: OT logical access removed same day; physical access badges deactivated; PAM accounts disabled
Investigate and document access anomalies by personnel — unusual hours, unusual systems accessed, unusual data volumes

governance.summary: OT personnel security procedures owned by HR with OT security input. Annual training records maintained. Access removal SLA enforced.
actions: OT personnel access list reviewed quarterly against HR
Departure/role-change access removal within 1 business day (same day for involuntary departures)
Annual training completion records maintained
Background screening records retained per HR policy

evidenceItems: Background screening policy and records (redacted for privacy)

slMapping.sl1: Basic security awareness training; access removed on departure
slMapping.sl2: Background screening; annual OT security training; quarterly access review; same-day removal on departure
slMapping.sl3: Enhanced screening for SIS/safety-critical roles; OT-specific training modules; monitored access patterns
slMapping.sl4: Continuous personnel vetting for safety system access; dual-person rules for SIS modifications; post-incident personnel investigation capability

requirements.name: Removable media and external device controls
requirements.description: Removable media (USB drives, laptops, tablets) are one of the most common OT attack vectors — used in the Stuxnet attack and many subsequent ICS compromises. Strict controls on what can be introduced to the OT network and how it is sanitised are essential.

legal.summary: IEC 62443 SR-2.3 covers mobile device management in OT; SR-3.2 covers protection from malicious code including media-borne malware. NACSA s18 requires measures appropriate to the sensitivity of NCII.

technical.summary: Whitelist-only USB policy for OT networks. All media scanned on air-gapped malware scanning workstation before introduction to OT network. Vendor laptops must use organisation-provided data transfer workstation or a clean laptop image.
actions: Implement USB port locking on all OT workstations (via BIOS, endpoint DLP, or physical USB locks) — allow only whitelisted devices
Deploy dedicated malware scanning workstation (not connected to OT network) for scanning all media before introduction to OT environment
Scanning workstation must have: offline threat signature updates, OT-relevant malware detection (ICS-specific malware signatures), and scan log capability
Vendor laptop policy: vendor laptops do not connect to OT network — data transferred via scanning workstation or organisation clean laptop only
OT portable device register: laptops and tablets used for OT work are registered assets, maintained by organisation, scanned before each OT use
Physical USB locks on SIS engineering workstations — two-person rule for SIS media handling
Log all removable media use on OT workstations (where endpoint monitoring is deployed)

governance.summary: Removable media policy enforced by technical controls. Exception requires OT security manager approval. Vendor media handling covered in vendor access policy.
actions: Removable media policy included in vendor access agreement
Annual audit of USB port lockdown on OT workstations
Scanning workstation maintenance record (signature updates, hardware check)
Exception log for approved removable media use

evidenceItems: Malware scanning workstation configuration and scan log records
Vendor access agreement including media handling clause

slMapping.sl2: USB policy documented; scanning workstation; vendor laptop policy
slMapping.sl3: Technical USB port locking; registered OT devices only; scan logs; vendor media procedure enforced
slMapping.sl4: Air-gapped malware scanning with OT-specific signatures; physical USB locks on SIS workstations; two-person media handling for SIS
